Detroit downtown people mover : environmental impact statement.

The Detroit Downtown People Mover (DPM) project proposed an automated transit system to improve circulation within Detroit's Central Business District. The system would consist of a 2.96-mile elevated loop with 13 stations, aimed at reducing traffic congestion, encouraging redevelopment, and improving air quality. The project was expected to stimulate economic growth by attracting new businesses and housing developments. However, it faced criticism for potential negative impacts on historic sites and existing businesses. The Southeastern Michigan Transportation Authority selected the DPM as the preferred alternative over a circulator bus system.

The Detroit Downtown People Mover was proposed to enhance transportation within the city's Central Business District, aiming to alleviate traffic congestion and boost economic development. While the project garnered support for its potential benefits, it also faced opposition due to concerns about its impact on historic sites and local businesses. Ultimately, the project was completed and has been operational since 1987, serving as a unique transit option in downtown Detroit.
United States. Urban Mass Transportation Administration
"The net effect of the proposed plans is to use premium land while encouraging what the Statement terms 'disinvestment' in the downtown area." — Thomas W. Reardon
"We view the DPM as a key transportation and development element in the revitalization of Detroit and its Central Business District." — City of Detroit Planning Department
